coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots from hemicucur[6]bit and the application for the detection of Pb2+
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Luo, Jia
Lei, Yao
Ge, Qingmei
Liu, Mao
Jiang, Nan
Huang, Yin-Hui
Cong, Hang
Zhao, Jiang-Lin
coredata.dc:description: A macrocyclic compound, hemicucurbit[6]uril (HemiQ[6]), is employed as the carbon source to produce a novel sort of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with blue fluorescence in aqueous solution. The CQDs are fully identified by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), X-ray diffraction (XRD), Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR), zeta potential, ultraviolet/visible (UV–vis) and photoluminescence spectroscopy (PL). The nanomaterial is developed for the analysis of Pb2+ in the light of the Resonance Rayleigh scattering (RRS) changes with the increasing Pb2+ concentration. The proposed probe emerges a high selectivity to Pb2+ and excellent sensitivity in the linear concentration range of 0–6 μM with a detection limit low to 0.42 μM, which is superior to the previous values of Pb2+ sensors, as well as the good anti-interference ability is confirmed by the specifical response to Pb2+ in the presence of other metal cations. Therefore, the proposed analysis of Pb2+ is explored for the application in real samples of tap water and lake water, in satisfied results of acceptable recoveries.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Hemicucurbituril
Macrocyclic compound
Resonance Rayleigh Scattering
Pb2+

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Photoluminescence assay and detection of Pb2+ by CQDs
Determination of Pb2+ in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) TEM of CQDs at 20 nm; b) size distribution histogram of the CQDs, the dimensions measured using Nano Measure software to select particles from TEM images; c) XRD pattern of CQDs; d) FT-IR spectrum of CQDs.
a) Complete XPS survey spectra of CQDs; b) C1s XPS spectrum; c) O1s XPS spectrum; d) N1s XPS spectrum.

a) UV–Vis absorption spectrum of CQDs, inset: the image of CQDs under the UV lamp; b) Fluorescence emission spectra of CQDs at different excitation wavelength; c) Fluorescence intensity of CQDs under different hydrothermal reaction time, λex/λem = 325/406 nm. The concentration of CQDs was 0.45 mg·mL−1 in ultrapure water.

a) The effect of pH on the fluorescence intensity of the CQDs; b) the effect of different concentrations of NaCl on fluorescence intensity of CQDs. The concentration of CQDs was 0.45 mg·mL−1 in ultrapure water, λex/λem = 325/406 nm.

a) Fluorescence changes of CQDs in the presence of different concentrations of Pb2+; b) plots of RRS intensity changes (ΔI RRS) of CQDs at 325 nm versus the concentrations of Pb2+; c) the corresponding linear calibration plots with different concentrations of Pb2+ (0–6 μM). The concentration of CQDs was 0.45 mg·mL−1 in ultrapure water with pH = 5.0 PBS buffer, λex/λem = 325/325 nm.

a) Competitive selectivity of the CQDs toward metal cations (all cation concentrations were 10 μM); b) interferences of the CQDs toward Pb2+ mixed with other metal ions; c) the reproducibility of the CQDs; d) the stability of the fluorescent probe. The concentration of CQDs was 0.45 mg·mL−1 in ultrapure water with pH = 5.0 PBS buffer, λex/λem = 325/325 nm.
Schematic diagram of the synthesis of CQDs and response of sensor to Pb2+.
Comparison of LOD for Pb2+ between the present sensor and the previous methods.
Recovery for detection of Pb2+ in real samples.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots from hemicucur[6]bit and the application for the detection of Pb2+

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Enterprise Technology Center of Guizhou Province, Guizhou University, Guiyang 550025, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Enterprise Technology Center of Guizhou Province

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Enterprise Technology Center of Guizhou Province, Guizhou University, Guiyang 550025, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Precision Medicine R&D Center, Zhuhai Institute of Advanced Technology, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Zhuhai 519080, Guangdong, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Precision Medicine R&D Center, Zhuhai Institute of Advanced Technology, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Zhuhai 519080, Guangdong, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A macrocyclic compound, hemicucurbit[6]uril (HemiQ[6]), is employed as the carbon source to produce a novel sort of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with blue fluorescence in aqueous solution. The CQDs are fully identified by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), X-ray diffraction (XRD), Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR), zeta potential, ultraviolet/visible (UV–vis) and photoluminescence spectroscopy (PL). The nanomaterial is developed for the analysis of Pb2+ in the light of the Resonance Rayleigh scattering (RRS) changes with the increasing Pb2+ concentration. The proposed probe emerges a high selectivity to Pb2+ and excellent sensitivity in the linear concentration range of 0–6 μM with a detection limit low to 0.42 μM, which is superior to the previous values of Pb2+ sensors, as well as the good anti-interference ability is confirmed by the specifical response to Pb2+ in the presence of other metal cations. Therefore, the proposed analysis of Pb2+ is explored for the application in real samples of tap water and lake water, in satisfied results of acceptable recoveries.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Lead (Pb) is a kind of toxic heavy metals, with the serious risks to human health and environment by body absorption and empoisoning water source and soil [1–3]. Even at a very low concentration in human body, Pb2+ seriously affects the brain and nervous system [2,4–8] to impair intelligence, lose memory, and decrease fertility [9,10]. Therefore, detection for trace amounts of Pb2+ is keenly demanded for development with high sensitivity and selectivity. The present analytic methods mainly include liquid chromatography [11], surface enhanced Raman spectroscopy [12], atomic absorption spectroscopy [13] and so on. However, the required equipment and technology are much complicated and expensive. Resonant Rayleigh scattering (RRS) is a phenomenon where the scattered light is greatly enhanced when the wavelength of the incident light is close to or located at the molecular absorption band [14]. RRS-based sensors and fluorescence sensors are widely used in ion detection and trace analysis of biomolecules for the advantages of fast detection, high sensitivity, easy operation, etc. [15–19]. Usually, the intensity of RRS is related to the size and number of scatters [14,20,21], and the performances of scattered light greatly depend upon the shape, composition and homogeneity of nanomaterials [22]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a new class of quasi-spherical and monodisperse nanoparticles in the diameters of less than 10 nm, perform the unique luminescent properties by photoexcitation or electroexcitation in the mechanism of bandgap transitions of the active electrons. The development of the carbon-base nanomaterials has been applied in the fields of nanoelectronics, optics, catalytic chemistry, biomedicine and sensors for the excellent properties including good water solubility, high quantum yield, good biocompatibility, high temperature stability, and low biological toxicity [23–27]. Notably, CQDs have shown promise in quantitatively detecting biomolecules [28] and metal ions [29–36]. Liu et al. [33] used chocolate as a raw material to prepare CDs via hydrothermal method, resulting in high selectivity for Pb2+ due to the chelation between Pb2+ and the hydroxyl groups on the surface of the CDs. Additionally, Kim et al. [31] reported that carbon dots (CDs) prepared using a hydrothermal method with L-lysine and L-glutathione as precursors exhibited a detection limit of 2.2 μM for Pb2+ and exhibited high stability under natural conditions. CQDs usually consist of carbon, oxygen, hydrogen, and nitrogen elements, and therefore the performances are determined by the components and size of the nanoparticles, that is, the different carbon sources, doping compositions and synthetic pathways provide different carbon quantum dots with the distinctive natures [34–36]. The CQDs in this article were prepared from hemicucurbit[6]uril (HemiQ[6], Scheme 1 ) as a precursor, with the sensitive response to Pb2+, and a new analytic method was built.

HemiQ[6] is a kind of macrocyclic compound in the cucurbituril [37–39] family, which was prepared in acidic medium by condensation of etheneourea and formaldehyde aqueous solution [40]. The unique flexible structure with carbonyl groups on the ports enabled organic molecules and inorganic ions to form supramolecular complexes [41,42], and the host–guest interactions were applied for molecular recognition [40] and supramolecular catalysis [43]. However, the application range of HemiQ[6] has been greatly reduced due to its lack of absorption in the ultraviolet region, difficulty in functionalization, and poor solubility [44,45]. As an organic macrocycle containing carbon, nitrogen, oxygen and hydrogen elements, HemiQ[6] was considered to be a new carbon source for preparation of a sort of water-soluble CQDs in this work. The CQDs prepared not only retained the macrocyclic structure of HemiQ[6], but also compensated for the aforementioned inherent drawbacks. Additionally, the CQDs exhibit blue luminescence when photoexcited. Therefore, a novel probe with high sensitivity and real-time response to Pb2+ in aqueous solution based on RRS was developed (Scheme 1).

Ethyleneurea, formaldehyde solution (37 %), HCl, NaCl, KCl, NiCl2·6H2O, FeCl3·6H2O, MgCl2·6H2O, PbCl2, HgCl2, CuCl2·2H2O, MnCl2·4H2O, ZnCl2·6H2O, CdCl2·2.5H2O and CaCl2·2H2O in analytical grade were obtained commercially and used without further purification. The reagents and chloride salts of metal ions were purchased from Beijing InnoKai Company, Aldrich and Alfa Aesar Chemical Reagent Company, and Chengdu Chemical Reagent Factory. All solutions were prepared with deionized water from Milli-Q ultrapure water system (DW, 18 MΩ cm−1).

1H NMR (Proton Nuclear Magnetic Resonance) and 13C NMR (Carbon Nuclear Magnetic Resonance) spectra were recorded on a JEOL JNM-ECZ400s spectrometer. UV–vis (Ultraviolet and visible) absorption were carried out on a Unico UV2500A. Fluorescence emission spectra were obtained on a Varian RF-540 fluorescence spectrophotometer. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was provided by a JEM-1400. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analyses was offered by a Thermo Scientific K-Alpha. X-ray diffractometry (XRD) analyses were performed on Thermo Scientific Smart Lab. Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR) were recorded on RT-LaTGS from BRER. DLS/zeta potential were recorded by Brookhaven.

HemiQ[6] was synthesized following the reported method [40] and characterized by 1H NMR and 13C NMR. As shown in Fig. S1-S2, there are two sets of characteristic proton signals at 3.43 ppm and 4.64 ppm, and three sets of characteristic carbon resonance peaks at 42.00 ppm, 56.46 ppm and 161.28 ppm, confirming the successful synthesis of HemiQ[6]. 58.8 mg HemiQ[6] was dissolved in 30 mL of ultrapure water, and then sonicated for 30 min for evenly dispersion. The mixture was transferred to a 50 mL reactor, and stood at 180 °C for 10 h, to produce a yellow liquid. The obtained liquid product was centrifuged at a speed of 10,000 rpm for 20 min, following by filtering with a 0.1 μm filter membrane and storing at 4 °C for further use. The concentration was calculated as 0.45 mg/mL by weighing method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photoluminescence assay and detection of Pb2+ by CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: In a typical method, the fluorescence was detected with 3 mL CQD solution in a quartz colorimetric dish. Different concentrations of Pb2+ solutions were added, and the luminescence spectra were recorded under excitation at 325 nm. All experiments were conducted at room temperature with routine incubation every 3 min. Then, metal ions (Cd2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Ca2+, Hg2+, Zn2+, K+, Na+, Fe3+) were added to afford the fluorescence intensities for the evaluation on selectivity and anti-interference abilities.

The morphology picture of the proposed CQDs in transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was collected in Fig. 1 a and Fig. S3, to reveal the well-distributed dots in about 2 nm. The particle size distribution histogram in Fig. 1b suggested the average size of the CQDs is 2.25 nm in the range of 1.26–3.49 nm. In addition, the dynamic light scattering (DLS) of CQDs obtained a size of about 2 nm in Fig. S4, which was consistent with the size of TEM. X-ray diffraction measurement was carried out for structural analysis, which revealed that the prepared CQDs exhibited an amorphous nature as shown in Fig. 1c, and the broad peak at 2θ = 28.4°, could be assigned to the (002) plane of graphite [46,47], that is, the as-synthesized CQDs exhibited both amorphous and graphitic natures. The surface functional groups of the CQDs were identified by FT-IR spectroscopy, as shown in Fig. 1d, the absorption band at 2954 cm−1 and 2883 cm−1 attributed to the stretching vibrations of −C–H, while the absorption peak at 1672 cm−1 was assigned to −CO [48], and the peaks at 1371 cm−1 and 1066 cm−1 were ascribed to −C-N and −C-O groups, respectively. The IR peak at 759 cm−1 indicated the exitance of −N–H group in the nanocomposite [49], and a peak at 1438 cm−1 was ascribed to –COO [50].

The chemical compositions of the prepared CQDs were measured by X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS), the existence of carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen elements was confirmed by the obvious peaks at 285.65, 531.71 and 401.3 eV (Fig. 2 a), corresponding to C1s, O1s and N1s, respectively. The high O content up to 42.88 %, which was calculated to be 16.31 % in the HemiQ[6] precursor, suggested the as-prepared CQDs were rich in oxygen-containing functional groups. The C1s peak was de-convoluted into four peaks at 284.5, 285.8, 286.3 and 288.3 eV as shown in Fig. 2b, which was assigned to C–C/CC, C-N, C-O and CO, respectively. The de-convolution of O1s peak into three peaks at 531.7, 533.17 and 535.8 eV as shown in Fig. 2c, indicated there were CO, C-OH, and water molecules. Two peaks at 399.6 and 401.3 eV were provided by the de-convolution of N1s peak as described in Fig. 2d, corresponding to C-N and N–H, respectively. Therefore, valence bond structures in HemiQ[6] were changed in the hydrothermal process. Significantly, the 1H NMR signal of CQDs corresponded to the original proton peak of HemiQ[6] (Fig. S5), indicating that the preservation of its rigid macrocyclic structure during the hydrothermal reaction process. Additionally, the zeta potential of CQDs in aqueous solution with different pH were measured. As displayed in Fig. S6, the zeta potential of CQDs shifted from positive to negative as the pH increased, with a range of 7.20 mV at pH = 2 to –23.01 mV at pH = 9, and an isoelectric point (pI) of 6.25. The CQDs exhibited a negative charge of −14.03 mV, which could be attributed to the negative charge of surface carboxyl groups. Although the surface charge increased with increasing pH values, the negative charge was relatively small due to the inhibitory effect of the surface amino groups [51–53].

The optical properties of the CQDs were evaluated with UV–vis absorption and photoluminescence spectroscopies. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the existence of the broad absorption peak in the range 258–291 nm implied the n-π* transition of −CO groups [54], and strong blue emission was produced under UV lamp irradiation (inset, Fig. 3a). The photoluminescence of the CQDs displayed great dependence on the excitation wavelength, that is, the fluorescence intensities were gradually improved and the emission wavelengths blue shifted with the excitation wavelength increasing, and emission reached the highest peak at 406 nm at 325 nm excitation, while the decreasing intensities and red shifted wavelength were observed with increased excitations from 350 nm to 400 nm (Fig. 3b and S7). On the other hand, the fluorescence intensity of the HemiQ[6] CQDs was related to the hydrothermal time, as the collection in Fig. 3c and S8, the fluorescence intensity at 325 nm excitation was improved with the increasing heated time, and reached the peak value in the optimal time of 10 hrs.

The stability of the CQDs was investigated in the pH range of 2–9 as shown in Fig. 4 . CQDs exhibited strong fluorescence emission under acidic conditions, which would be the protonation of carbonyl, amino, or CN groups on CQDs. Excessive acidity saturated the protonation, resulting in no further increase in fluorescence intensity. However, under alkaline conditions, possible deprotonation of functional groups led to decreased fluorescence intensity (Fig. 4a) [55,56]. The ionic strength experiments demonstrated that the CQDs exhibited good stability, with no significant changes in fluorescence intensity observed in NaCl solutions of different concentrations, even in over an hour in high ion strength solutions (Fig. 4b).

The evaluation on analytic selectivity of the developed CQDs was performed by employment of a variety of metal cations including Ca2+, Cu2+, K+, Mg2+, Na+, Ni2+, Mn2+, Cd2+, Hg2+ and Zn2+ (Fig. S12), and the columns in Fig. 6 a indicated no significant response to other metal cations, in results of high selectivity for Pb2+ detection of the proposed sensor. The anti-interference ability of the constructed method was estimated by adding twentyfold concentration of interfering cations (Ca2+, Cu2+, K+, Mg2+, Na+, Ni2+, Mn2+, Cd2+, Hg2+ and Zn2+) (Fig. S13) to the mixed solution of Pb2+ and CQDs, as shown in Fig. 6b, the Fluorescence intensity kept a constant in the presence of interferences, suggesting a high specificity to Pb2+. Five groups of the CQDs solutions were severally subjected to the determination of Pb2+ under the same conditions (Fig. S14), and a value of 1.35 % RSD was obtained (Fig. 6c), therefore, the repeatability of the developed analysis was satisfied. The CQDs solution was quietly standing for 5 days, and performed the test of Pb2+ at an interval of a day (Fig. S15). The statistic offered the relative standard deviation (RSD) of 1.21 % (Fig. 6d), indicating the good stability of the as-prepared CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Pb2+ in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Since Pb2+ played a lethal role in the water environment, the determination was performed in two different water sources, tap water and lake water. The practical applicability of the prepared CQDs for Pb2+ detection was evaluated with recovery experiments. The results from standard addition method were collected in Table 2 , which indicated that the corresponding recoveries were from 96.50 % to 115.01 % in the linear Pb2+ concentration range from 0 to 6 μM. The results suggested the potential application of the developed detection method for the detection of Pb2+ in real water.

In summary, the macrocyclic hemicucurbit[6]uril containing C, N, O, and H elements, is explored to fabricate CQDs by the facile hydrothermal treatment, with good blue-luminescence and water-dispersibility, and retaining the rigid macrocyclic skeleton of HemiQ[6], the nanomaterial is developed for sensing Pb2+ with high selectivity and sensitivity based on RRS. The specific response to Pb2+ is achieved in the coexistence of the interferences involving Ca2+, Cu2+, K+, Mg2+, Na+, Ni2+, Mn2+, Cd2+, Hg2+ and Zn2+. A detection limit of 0.42 μM is obtained in the linear concentration range of 0–6 μM, confirming the advantage of the proposed sensor. The constructed analysis with HemiQ[6] CQDs is applied for the efficient determination of Pb2+ in real water samples. The discovery in this work explores an approach to prepare CQDs from HemiQ[6], which is employed for the analysis of Pb2+ in aqueous solution in efficiency.

Jia Luo: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Yao Lei: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation. Qingmei Ge: Investigation, Data curation. Mao Liu: Investigation, Data curation. Nan Jiang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Yin-Hui Huang: Writing – review & editing, Data curation. Hang Cong: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Jiang-Lin Zhao: Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22262007), High-level Talent Project of Guizhou Province (No. GCC[2023]010), Guangdong Basic and Applied Basic Research Foundation (No. 2023A1515010832).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2024.124459.
